f8990c8d79e9b4ac.summary: Light reflects obeying laws: angle of incidence = angle of reflection, incident/reflected/normal in same plane. Regular (smooth surface) vs diffuse (rough surface) reflection.

chunks.title: Understanding Reflection of Light
chunks.content:
**Reflection of Light** is a key topic in Light – Reflection and Refraction for CBSE Class X Science.

**Light:** Form of energy that enables vision. Travels in straight lines (rectilinear propagation). Speed: 3×10⁸ m/s in vacuum.

**Reflection:** Bouncing back of light when it strikes a surface.

**Laws of Reflection:**
1. Incident ray, reflected ray, and normal all lie in the SAME plane
2. Angle of incidence (∠i) = Angle of reflection (∠r)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Types of Reflection
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Regular/Specular reflection:** Smooth surface (mirror, calm water). Parallel incident rays → parallel reflected rays. Clear image formed
• **Diffuse/Irregular reflection:** Rough surface (paper, wall, cloth). Parallel incident rays → reflected in different directions. No image. Enables us to see non-luminous objects from any angle

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Image Formation by Plane Mirror
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Characteristics:** Virtual, erect, same size as object, laterally inverted (left↔right). Distance behind mirror = distance in front
• **Field of view:** Larger than that of a window of same size (can see more area)
• **Uses:** Periscope, kaleidoscope, looking glass, sign boards (message reversed on ambulance)

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A person stands 2 m in front of a plane mirror. Where is the image? What is the distance between person and image?

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Image formed behind mirror at same distance = 2 m
**Step 2:** Image is virtual, erect, same size
**Step 3:** Distance between person and image = 2+2 = 4 m

**Answer:** Image 2 m behind mirror. Distance = 4 m.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** State laws of reflection.

**Answer:** ∠i = ∠r. Incident, reflected, normal in same plane.

---

**Q2.** Why can't we see our image in a wall?

**Answer:** Wall is rough → diffuse reflection. Light scatters in all directions, no image formed.

---

**Q3.** Why is the word AMBULANCE written inverted on vehicles?

**Answer:** Mirror reflection reverses. Driver ahead reads AMBULANCE correctly in rearview mirror.

24567c8c0fcac90b.summary: Spherical mirrors (concave/convex). Terms: pole, centre of curvature, focus, focal length (f=R/2). Image formation rules: ray diagrams for object at various positions. Uses: concave (shaving mirror, solar cooker), convex (rearview mirror). Mirror equation: 1/f = 1/u + 1/v. Magnification: m = -v/u.

chunks.title: Understanding Spherical Mirrors
chunks.content:
**Spherical Mirrors** is a key topic in Light – Reflection and Refraction for CBSE Class X Science.

**Spherical mirror:** Part of a hollow sphere.

**Types:**
• **Concave (converging):** Reflecting surface curves INWARD. Focus in front
• **Convex (diverging):** Reflecting surface curves OUTWARD. Focus behind

**Terms:** Pole (P), Centre of curvature (C), Radius of curvature (R), Focus/Focal point (F), Focal length (f) = R/2

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Image Formation: Ray Diagrams
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Rules for concave mirror:** (1) Ray ∥ to axis → passes through F after reflection. (2) Ray through F → ∥ to axis. (3) Ray through C → reflects back along same path. (4) Ray to pole P → reflects with ∠i=∠r
• **Concave — Object at:** ∞ → real, inverted, diminished at F. Beyond C → real, inverted, diminished between C and F. At C → real, inverted, same size at C. Between C and F → real, inverted, enlarged beyond C. At F → real, inverted, highly enlarged at ∞. Between P and F → virtual, erect, enlarged behind mirror
• **Convex mirror:** Always produces virtual, erect, diminished image. Used for rearview mirrors (wider field of view)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Mirror Formula and Magnification
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Mirror formula:** 1/f = 1/u + 1/v. Sign convention: u = -ve (object in front). f = -ve (concave), +ve (convex). v = -ve (real image), +ve (virtual image)
• **Magnification (m):** m = hᵢ/hₒ = -v/u. |m|>1 → enlarged; |m|<1 → diminished; |m|=1 → same size. Positive m = virtual/erect; negative m = real/inverted

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** An object 5 cm high is placed 15 cm from a concave mirror of focal length 10 cm. Find position, nature, and size of image.

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Given: u = -15 cm, f = -10 cm, hₒ = 5 cm
**Step 2:** 1/v = 1/f - 1/u = -1/10 + 1/15 = (-3+2)/30 = -1/30
**Step 3:** v = -30 cm (real image, 30 cm in front of mirror)
**Step 4:** m = -v/u = -(-30)/(-15) = -2 (inverted, enlarged)
**Step 5:** hᵢ = m×hₒ = -2×5 = -10 cm (10 cm, inverted)

**Answer:** Image: real, inverted, 10 cm tall, 30 cm in front of mirror.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** What is the focal length of a concave mirror with R=40 cm?

**Answer:** f = R/2 = 20 cm.

---

**Q2.** Why are convex mirrors used as rearview mirrors?

**Answer:** Wider field of view, always erect image, shows traffic behind.

---

**Q3.** An object placed 20 cm from a concave mirror gives real image at 20 cm. Find f.

**Answer:** 1/f = 1/20+1/20 = 2/20 = 1/10. f = 10 cm.

03d99857ba348019.summary: Refraction: bending of light when passing from one medium to another. Light slows down in denser medium. Laws: Snell's law (n₁ sin i = n₂ sin r). Refractive index n = c/v. Examples: apparent depth, stick in water appearing bent.

chunks.title: Understanding Refraction of Light
chunks.content:
**Refraction of Light** is a key topic in Light – Reflection and Refraction for CBSE Class X Science.

**Refraction:** The bending of light when it passes obliquely from one transparent medium to another.

**Cause:** Light changes speed when entering a different medium.

**Effects:**
• Rarer→Denser: bends TOWARDS the normal (slows down)
• Denser→Rarer: bends AWAY from the normal (speeds up)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Laws of Refraction (Snell's Law)
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Law 1:** Incident ray, refracted ray, and normal at interface all lie in same plane
• **Law 2 (Snell's Law):** n₁ sin i = n₂ sin r, where n = refractive index, i = angle of incidence, r = angle of refraction
• **Refractive index:** n = speed of light in vacuum / speed of light in medium = c/v. Always ≥1. Higher n = optically denser medium
• **Relative refractive index:** n₂₁ = n₂/n₁ (from medium 1 to 2)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Effects of Refraction in Daily Life
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Apparent depth:** Object in water appears closer to surface (less deep) due to refraction. Real depth / Apparent depth = n (refractive index of water ≈ 4/3)
• **Stick in water appears bent:** Light from underwater part refracts at water-air interface → appears at different angle
• **Twinkling of stars:** Atmospheric refraction of starlight (continuous change in air density → light path keeps changing)
• **Sun visible before sunrise/after sunset:** Atmospheric refraction bends sunlight → we see sun ~2 min before actual sunrise

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Light enters from air to water (n=4/3). If angle of incidence is 45°, find angle of refraction. (sin 45°=0.707)

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** n₁ sin i = n₂ sin r
**Step 2:** 1 × 0.707 = (4/3) × sin r
**Step 3:** sin r = 0.707 × 3/4 = 0.530
**Step 4:** r = sin⁻¹(0.530) ≈ 32°

**Answer:** r ≈ 32° (light bends towards normal, water is denser).

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** What is the speed of light in glass if n=1.5 and c=3×10⁸ m/s?

**Answer:** v = c/n = 3×10⁸/1.5 = 2×10⁸ m/s.

---

**Q2.** Why does a pencil appear bent when partly immersed in water?

**Answer:** Light from underwater part refracts at water-air interface → appears at different position.

---

**Q3.** If n of diamond is 2.42, what is the speed of light in diamond?

**Answer:** v = 3×10⁸/2.42 ≈ 1.24×10⁸ m/s.

96fe99dec7d3d34d.summary: Light bends when entering/exiting a rectangular glass slab. Emergent ray is parallel to incident ray (laterally displaced). Displacement depends on thickness, refractive index, angle of incidence.

chunks.title: Understanding Refraction through a Rectangular Glass Slab
chunks.content:
**Refraction through a Rectangular Glass Slab** is a key topic in Light – Reflection and Refraction for CBSE Class X Science.

**Experiment:** Pass a ray of light through a rectangular glass slab.

**Observations:**
1. Incident ray bends towards normal at air→glass interface
2. Ray travels straight inside glass
3. Emergent ray bends away from normal at glass→air interface
4. Emergent ray is PARALLEL to incident ray (same direction)
5. Lateral displacement occurs (lateral shift)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Lateral Displacement
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Lateral shift:** The perpendicular distance between the path of incident ray and emergent ray
• **Factors:** Depends on thickness of slab (more thickness = more shift), refractive index (higher n = more shift), angle of incidence (higher i = more shift)
• **Why no deviation of direction?** Light enters and exits through parallel surfaces. Air→Glass: bends towards normal. Glass→Air: bends away from normal by same amount. Net angular deviation = 0

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Verifying Laws of Refraction
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Procedure:** Place glass slab on paper. Draw outline. Draw incident ray (angle i). Pin pricks to trace path. Draw emergent ray. Measure i, r, e. Observe i = e (parallel). Measure lateral shift (d)
• **Results confirm:** Snell's law (n₁ sin i = n₂ sin r). Light bends towards normal in denser medium. Reversibility of light (path is reversible)

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A ray incident at 40° on a glass slab (n=1.5, thickness 5 cm) emerges parallel. What is the angle of emergence? Explain.

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** At first surface: 1×sin40° = 1.5×sinr → sinr = 0.429 → r ≈ 25.4°
**Step 2:** Ray travels through glass (straight)
**Step 3:** At second surface: 1.5×sin25.4° = 1×sine → sine = 0.643 → e = 40°
**Step 4:** e = i (40°). Emergent ray ∥ incident ray

**Answer:** Angle of emergence = 40° (same as incidence). Emergent ray is parallel to incident ray.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** Why is the emergent ray parallel to the incident ray in a glass slab?

**Answer:** Both surfaces are parallel. The bending at entry and exit are equal and opposite (net deviation zero).

---

**Q2.** What is lateral displacement?

**Answer:** Perpendicular distance between incident and emergent ray paths. Increases with slab thickness and refractive index.

---

**Q3.** If angle of incidence is 0° (normal incidence), what happens?

**Answer:** No bending (i=r=0). Ray goes straight through without deviation or lateral shift.

eb14e744b7e4bae5.summary: Refractive index (n) = c/v. Absolute (vacuum→medium) vs Relative (medium1→medium2). Higher n = optically denser. Snell's law: n₁ sin i = n₂ sin r. Applications: optical fibres, lenses, apparent depth.

chunks.title: Understanding Refractive Index
chunks.content:
**Refractive Index** is a key topic in Light – Reflection and Refraction for CBSE Class X Science.

**Refractive index (n):** Measure of how much light slows down in a medium.

n = Speed of light in vacuum (or air) / Speed of light in medium = c/v

**Examples:**
Air n≈1.0003, Water n=4/3≈1.33, Glass n=1.5, Diamond n=2.42

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Types of Refractive Index
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Absolute refractive index:** n = c/v. Refers to vacuum as reference. For air, n ≈ 1 (very close to vacuum)
• **Relative refractive index:** n₂₁ = n₂/n₁ = v₁/v₂. From medium 1 to medium 2. Used in Snell's law calculations
• **Optical density vs physical density:** Higher n does not necessarily mean higher physical density (e.g., oil vs water)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Applications of Refractive Index
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Total Internal Reflection (TIR):** When light goes from denser→rarer medium at angle > critical angle (C). sin C = n_rarer / n_denser. All light reflected back
• **Optical fibres:** Light guided by repeated TIR inside glass fibre. Used in telecommunications, endoscopy
• **Diamond sparkle:** High n=2.42 → very small critical angle (≈24°) → light undergoes multiple internal reflections → intense sparkle
• **Lens maker:** n determines lens power. Different lens materials (crown glass n=1.52, flint glass n=1.62) give different dispersion

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Critical angle of diamond is 24°. What is its refractive index? (sin 24° = 0.407)

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** sin C = n_air / n_diamond
**Step 2:** sin 24° = 1 / n_diamond
**Step 3:** 0.407 = 1 / n_diamond
**Step 4:** n_diamond = 1/0.407 ≈ 2.46

**Answer:** n ≈ 2.46. (Actual value 2.42; small difference from sin approximation).

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** What is the refractive index of water? What does it mean?

**Answer:** n=4/3≈1.33. Light travels 1.33 times slower in water than in vacuum.

---

**Q2.** What is the critical angle for water-air interface? (n_water=1.33)

**Answer:** sin C = 1/1.33 = 0.75 → C = sin⁻¹(0.75) ≈ 48.75°.

---

**Q3.** Why do diamonds sparkle?

**Answer:** High n (2.42) → small critical angle (~24°) → light undergoes many internal reflections before emerging.

77609e640fad4330.summary: Lenses (convex/converging, concave/diverging). Terms: optical centre, principal focus, focal length. Ray diagrams. Lens formula: 1/f = 1/v - 1/u. Power: P = 1/f (dioptre D). Magnification: m = v/u.

chunks.title: Understanding Refraction by Spherical Lenses
chunks.content:
**Refraction by Spherical Lenses** is a key topic in Light – Reflection and Refraction for CBSE Class X Science.

**Lens:** Transparent material (usually glass) with two spherical surfaces.

• **Convex (converging):** Thicker at centre. Focuses parallel light to a point. f positive
• **Concave (diverging):** Thinner at centre. Spreads parallel light. f negative

**Terms:** Optical centre (O), Principal focus (F), Focal length (f)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Image Formation by Lenses
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Convex lens — Object:** Beyond 2F → real, inverted, diminished between F and 2F. At 2F → real, inverted, same size at 2F. Between F and 2F → real, inverted, enlarged beyond 2F. At F → real, inverted, highly enlarged at ∞. Between O and F → virtual, erect, enlarged (magnifier)
• **Concave lens:** Always produces virtual, erect, diminished image for any object position (between O and F)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Lens Formula and Power
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Lens formula:** 1/f = 1/v - 1/u. Sign convention: u = -ve (object in front). f = +ve (convex), -ve (concave). v = +ve (real image behind lens), -ve (virtual image in front)
• **Magnification (m):** m = hᵢ/hₒ = v/u. |m|>1 → enlarged; <1 → diminished. Positive m = virtual/erect; negative m = real/inverted
• **Power (P):** P = 1/f (in metres). Unit: Dioptre (D). Convex lens: P positive. Concave lens: P negative

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** An object 4 cm high is placed 20 cm from a convex lens of focal length 15 cm. Find position, nature, size of image.

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Given: u = -20 cm, f = +15 cm, hₒ = 4 cm
**Step 2:** 1/v = 1/f + 1/u = 1/15 + 1/(-20) = (4-3)/60 = 1/60
**Step 3:** v = 60 cm (real image, behind lens)
**Step 4:** m = v/u = 60/(-20) = -3 (inverted, enlarged)
**Step 5:** hᵢ = m×4 = -12 cm (12 cm, inverted)

**Answer:** Image: real, inverted, 12 cm tall, 60 cm behind lens.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** What is the power of a convex lens of focal length 25 cm?

**Answer:** P = 1/0.25 = +4 D.

---

**Q2.** What type of lens always produces a virtual, erect, diminished image?

**Answer:** Concave lens (for any object position).

---

**Q3.** A concave lens of focal length 20 cm. Where is the image of an object 30 cm away?

**Answer:** 1/v = 1/f + 1/u = -1/20 + 1/(-30) = -5/60 = -1/12. v = -12 cm (virtual, in front).

1187a8c10f70052f.summary: Human eye: cornea (bends light), iris/pupil (controls light), lens (focuses), retina (image formation → signals to brain via optic nerve). Accommodation: ciliary muscles adjust lens shape for near/far objects (power of accommodation).

chunks.title: Understanding The Human Eye
chunks.content:
**The Human Eye** is a key topic in The Human Eye and the Colourful World for CBSE Class X Science.

The human eye is a complex optical instrument:

**Structure:** Cornea (transparent front) → Aqueous humour → Iris (coloured, controls pupil size) → Pupil (light aperture) → Lens (biconvex, elastic) → Vitreous humour → Retina (photoreceptor cells) → Optic nerve → Brain

**Image formation:** Lens forms real, inverted image on retina. Brain interprets as erect.

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Working of the Eye
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Cornea:** Does ~2/3 of light bending (fixed refraction). Lens provides fine adjustment (variable focus)
• **Iris and Pupil:** Iris muscles control pupil diameter. Bright light → pupil constricts (protects retina). Dim light → pupil dilates (more light enters)
• **Accommodation:** Ciliary muscles change lens shape (thickness). Thicker lens = more converging = near objects. Thinner lens = less converging = far objects. Power of accommodation = range of adjustment
• **Near point:** ~25 cm for normal adult eye. **Far point:** Infinity. Range of vision = near point to infinity

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Persistance of Vision and Other Properties
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Persistence of vision:** Image remains on retina for ~1/16th second after object removed. Movies displayed at 24 frames/sec → appears continuous
• **Blind spot:** Where optic nerve leaves retina — no photoreceptors. Eye fills in gap using context
• **Colour vision:** Retina has cones (colour, bright light) and rods (black/white, dim light). ~120 million rods, ~6 million cones 🌈

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** How does the eye focus on objects at different distances?

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** For far objects: ciliary muscles relaxed → lens thin (small curvature) → less converging → image on retina
**Step 2:** For near objects: ciliary muscles contract → lens thick (more curvature) → more converging → image on retina
**Step 3:** Process called 'accommodation'. Lens elasticity decreases with age (presbyopia)

**Answer:** Ciliary muscles change lens thickness → changes focal length → maintains sharp image on retina.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** What is the function of the iris?

**Answer:** Controls pupil size (dilates/constricts) to regulate amount of light entering eye.

---

**Q2.** What is the near point and far point of a normal eye?

**Answer:** Near point = 25 cm. Far point = infinity.

---

**Q3.** Why do we see a movie as continuous motion?

**Answer:** Persistence of vision (~1/16 s). Successive frames merge seamlessly (24 fps).

20f1b159774bed6b.summary: Myopia (nearsightedness): image before retina → concave lens. Hypermetropia (farsightedness): image behind retina → convex lens. Presbyopia: age-related loss of accommodation → bifocals. Astigmatism: irregular cornea → cylindrical lens. Cataract: lens cloudy → surgery.

chunks.title: Understanding Defects of Vision and their Correction
chunks.content:
**Defects of Vision and their Correction** is a key topic in The Human Eye and the Colourful World for CBSE Class X Science.

**Common vision defects and their correction:**

1. **Myopia (Nearsightedness):** Cannot see distant objects clearly
2. **Hypermetropia (Farsightedness):** Cannot see nearby objects clearly
3. **Presbyopia:** Age-related, cannot see nearby objects

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Myopia and Hypermetropia
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Myopia:** Eyeball too long OR lens too curved. Image formed BEFORE retina. Can see near, not far. Corrected by **concave lens** (diverges light before it enters eye). Power: -ve sign
• **Hypermetropia:** Eyeball too short OR lens too flat. Image formed BEHIND retina. Can see far, not near. Corrected by **convex lens** (converges light more). Power: +ve sign

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Presbyopia, Astigmatism, and Cataract
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Presbyopia:** Age-related (>40 yrs). Lens loses elasticity → can't accommodate for near objects. Corrected by **bifocal lenses** (upper part for distance: concave/convex; lower part for reading: convex in both cases)
• **Astigmatism:** Irregular curvature of cornea/lens → blurred vision at ALL distances. Corrected by **cylindrical lenses** (different power in different meridians)
• **Cataract:** Lens becomes cloudy (opaque). Not a 'defect' but a medical condition. Corrected by **surgery** (remove cloudy lens, implant artificial intraocular lens)

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A person cannot see objects beyond 1 m clearly. What defect does they have? Find lens power.

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Cannot see far objects → myopia (nearsightedness)
**Step 2:** Far point = 1 m (normal = ∞)
**Step 3:** Need concave lens that makes object at ∞ appear at 1 m
**Step 4:** 1/f = 1/v - 1/u = 1/(-1) - 1/∞ = -1
**Step 5:** P = -1 D (concave lens)

**Answer:** Myopia. Corrected by concave lens of power -1 D.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** What type of lens corrects hypermetropia? Why?

**Answer:** Convex lens. Converges light more so that image falls ON the retina.

---

**Q2.** What is presbyopia? How is it corrected?

**Answer:** Age-related loss of accommodation. Corrected by bifocal lenses.

---

**Q3.** A person needs +2 D lens. What defect do they have?

**Answer:** Hypermetropia (+2 D = convex lens, f=50 cm). Cannot see near objects clearly.

af7724cf41683a3d.summary: Light passing through a triangular prism bends twice (refraction at both surfaces), causing overall deviation. Dispersion: white light splits into 7 colours (VIBGYOR). Violet bends most, red least. Prism formula: δ = i₁ + i₂ - A.

chunks.title: Understanding Refraction of Light through a Prism
chunks.content:
**Refraction of Light through a Prism** is a key topic in The Human Eye and the Colourful World for CBSE Class X Science.

**Prism:** Transparent medium bounded by two plane surfaces inclined at an angle (refracting angle A).

**Path of light:**
1. Air→Glass: bends TOWARDS normal (slows down)
2. Travels through prism
3. Glass→Air: bends AWAY from normal (speeds up)
**Net result:** Light deviates towards the BASE of the prism

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Angle of Deviation and Dispersion
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Angle of deviation (δ):** The angle between incident ray and emergent ray. Depends on A (prism angle), n (refractive index), and angle of incidence
• **Minimum deviation (δₘ):** When incident angle = emergent angle. Prism formula: n = sin[(A+δₘ)/2] / sin(A/2)
• **Dispersion:** White light splits into constituent colours (VIBGYOR) because different colours have different speeds in glass → different bending. Violet (most deviation, shortest λ). Red (least deviation, longest λ)

0c2e1cdaa71a6264.summary: Dispersion: white light splits into 7 colours (VIBGYOR) through a prism. Rainbow: natural dispersion by water droplets (refraction + internal reflection + refraction). Newton showed white light is composed of colours; two prisms recombine colours to white.

chunks.title: Understanding Dispersion of White Light by a Glass Prism
chunks.content:
**Dispersion of White Light by a Glass Prism** is a key topic in The Human Eye and the Colourful World for CBSE Class X Science.

**Isaac Newton (1666):** Showed that white light is composed of seven colours.

**Dispersion:** Splitting of white light into its constituent colours.

**Order:** Violet → Indigo → Blue → Green → Yellow → Orange → Red (VIBGYOR)

**Reason:** Different colours have different wavelengths → different speeds in glass → different refractive indices → bend by different amounts.

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Rainbow Formation and Recombination
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Rainbow:** Natural spectrum formed by dispersion in water droplets. Three steps: Refraction at entry, Total Internal Reflection inside droplet, Refraction at exit. Sun behind observer → rainbow in opposite direction
• **Recombination:** Two prisms placed opposite. First prism splits, second prism recombines colours back to white light. Proves white light = mixture of coloured lights
• **Why VIBGYOR?** Violet has shortest wavelength (400 nm) → bends most. Red has longest wavelength (700 nm) → bends least

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Newton placed a second prism inverted after the first. What did he observe and why?

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** First prism: white light → spectrum (VIBGYOR) — dispersion
**Step 2:** Second prism (inverted): spectrum → white light — recombination
**Step 3:** Conclusion: White light is composed of 7 colours
**Step 4:** Prisms do not 'add colour' — they separate existing colours

**Answer:** Spectrum recombined to white light. Proves white light = mix of all colours.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** Name the seven colours of the spectrum in order.

**Answer:** Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, Red (VIBGYOR).

---

**Q2.** Why does violet bend more than red through a prism?

**Answer:** Violet has shorter wavelength → slower in glass → higher refractive index → more bending.

---

**Q3.** How is a rainbow formed?

**Answer:** Sunlight → water droplets → refraction (enters) → TIR (inside) → refraction (exits) → dispersion → rainbow.

8493f1f7a882c4a6.summary: Atmospheric refraction: light bends due to varying air density/temperature. Effects: twinkling of stars (turbulence), sun visible before sunrise/after sunset (~2 min early), apparent flattening of sun at horizon, mirages (hot air near ground).

chunks.title: Understanding Atmospheric Refraction
chunks.content:
**Atmospheric Refraction** is a key topic in The Human Eye and the Colourful World for CBSE Class X Science.

**Atmospheric refraction:** The refraction of light by Earth's atmosphere due to varying air density (and hence varying refractive index) at different heights.

**Key effects:**
• Stars twinkle
• Sun visible before actual sunrise and after sunset
• Sun appears flattened at sunrise/sunset
• Mirages

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Phenomena Explained by Atmospheric Refraction
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Twinkling of stars:** Light from star enters atmosphere → bends due to changing air density. Turbulence (moving air pockets) causes light path to fluctuate → apparent position and brightness vary. Planets (extended discs) don't twinkle much (average of many light rays)
• **Advance sunrise/delayed sunset:** Sun appears ~2 min before actual sunrise and remains visible ~2 min after actual sunset. Light from below-horizon sun bends around Earth's curvature due to atmospheric refraction
• **Apparent flattening:** At sunrise/sunset, lower edge of sun is refracted more than upper edge → sun appears elliptical (flattened)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Mirages
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Mirage (in deserts):** Hot air near ground has lower density (lower n) than cooler air above. Light from sky bends UPWARDS (away from normal as it goes from denser→rarer). Apparent reflection of sky on ground → looks like water
• **Not an illusion:** The bend light path is real. The 'water' is an image of the sky. Similar phenomenon on hot roads (looks wet)

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Why do stars twinkle but planets do not?

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Stars are point sources (very far, appear as points of light)
**Step 2:** Atmospheric turbulence bends light from different parts of the same star differently → fluctuations in brightness
**Step 3:** Planets are extended discs (not points). Light from different points on disc average out the fluctuations
**Step 4:** Result: planets appear steady, stars twinkle

**Answer:** Stars = point sources → turbulence effects visible. Planets = extended discs → effects averaged out.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** Why is the sun visible before actual sunrise?

**Answer:** Atmospheric refraction bends sunlight from below horizon → appears above horizon ~2 min early.

---

**Q2.** What causes a mirage in the desert?

**Answer:** Hot air near ground (less dense) → light bends upward → looks like water reflection of sky.

---

**Q3.** Why does the sun appear flattened at sunrise?

**Answer:** Lower edge refracts more than upper edge → elliptical appearance.

95fb5c3ff2484dbb.summary: Scattering of light by particles smaller than wavelength. Rayleigh scattering (intensity ∝ 1/λ⁴): blue sky (shorter λ violet/blue scattered more), red sunrise/sunset (longer path, blue scattered away, red reaches eye). Tyndall effect: colloid particles scatter light.

chunks.title: Understanding Scattering of Light
chunks.content:
**Scattering of Light** is a key topic in The Human Eye and the Colourful World for CBSE Class X Science.

**Scattering:** Absorption and re-emission of light by particles.

**Rayleigh scattering:** Scattering intensity ∝ 1/λ⁴ (shorter wavelengths scatter much more).

This explains:
1. Blue colour of sky
2. Red/ orange colour of sunrise/sunset
3. White colour of clouds
4. Tyndall effect

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Colour of Sky and Sun
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Blue sky:** Shorter wavelengths (violet, blue) scatter more by air molecules. Our eyes are more sensitive to blue than violet → sky appears blue. In space (no atmosphere), sky appears black (no scattering)
• **Red sunrise/sunset:** Sun near horizon → light travels longer through atmosphere. Most blue/violet scattered away (multiple scattering). Only red/orange (longer λ, less scattered) reaches our eyes → red/orange sun
• **White clouds:** Water droplets in clouds are larger than wavelength. They scatter ALL colours equally (Mie scattering) → clouds appear white

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Tyndall Effect
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Tyndall effect:** Scattering of light by colloidal particles (larger than molecules, smaller than visible). Particles must be ~1-100 nm
• **Examples:** Blue colour of sky (dust+water in atmosphere). Headlight beam in fog. Blue eyes vs brown: more scattering in blue (less pigment). Milk, starch solution, smoke show Tyndall effect
• **Not Tyndall:** True solutions (salt water, sugar water) do NOT show Tyndall effect (particles too small)

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Why does the sky appear dark to astronauts on the moon?

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Moon has NO atmosphere (no air molecules to scatter light)
**Step 2:** Without scattering, the sky remains black (even when sun is shining)
**Step 3:** This proves blue colour of sky is from atmospheric scattering

**Answer:** No atmosphere → no scattering → sky appears black even in sunlight.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** Why is the sky blue?

**Answer:** Shorter wavelengths (blue/violet) scatter more by air molecules (Rayleigh scattering ∝ 1/λ⁴).

---

**Q2.** Why is the sun red at sunset?

**Answer:** Longer path through atmosphere → blue scattered away, only red reaches eye.

---

**Q3.** What is the Tyndall effect? Give two examples.

**Answer:** Scattering by colloidal particles. Examples: beam of light in fog/milk, blue colour of eyes with less pigment.
